1: Tongues of men and of angels: the tradition of angelic languages appears in Jewish apocalyptic — the Testament of Job 48-50 describes the daughters of Job speaking in angelic dialects after receiving heavenly sashes. The Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice from Qumran describe angelic liturgy. Paul may be granting the Corinthians their highest possible claim for tongues to show that even this is worthless without love.
2: Move mountains: the hyperbolic faith that moves mountains echoes the dominical saying (Matthew 17:20; 1 Corinthians 21:21). The allusion places Paul's teaching in line with Jesus's own sayings about faith and makes the illustration recognizable to those who knew the Jesus tradition.
3: Give away all possessions and surrender the body: the two extremes of material self-denial — total charity and ultimate self-sacrifice. Both were recognizable ideals in the ancient world: Cynic philosophers practiced radical poverty; martyrdom was the ultimate self-giving. Paul says even these avail nothing without agapē.
4: Patience (makrothymia) and kindness (chrēstotēs): the two opening attributes describe agapē in relational terms — patience with people rather than circumstances (makrothymia, longsuffering) and active kindness toward them. Both appear in the fruit of the Spirit list (Galatians 5:22) and are used of God's own character (Romans 2:4).
5: Love does not seek its own (ta heautēs): the selflessness of agapē directly counters the Corinthian pattern of seeking one's own advantage in spiritual gifts, in food, and in litigation. The phrase echoes Paul's own practice described in 1 Corinthians 10:33 (not seeking my own advantage).
6: Love does not rejoice in wrongdoing but in truth: the pairing of wrongdoing (adikia) and truth (alētheia) has philosophical resonance — in Greek ethics, adikia was the fundamental vice of injustice, and alētheia was the virtue of honesty that underpins all right relations. Love refuses to take pleasure in another's failure or the triumph of injustice.
7: Bears, believes, hopes, endures all things: the four stegei/pisteuei/elpizei/hypomenei verbs create a climactic list. Stegō (bear, cover) appears in the context of not exposing others' failings; hypomenō (endure, remain under) was the Stoic virtue of patient perseverance under hardship. Love incorporates and transcends the Stoic ideals.
8: Love never fails (agapē oudepote piptei): piptō (fall, fail, come to an end) — love will not fall or come to nothing at the eschaton when all partial gifts cease. The three gifts named — prophecy, tongues, knowledge — are precisely the ones the Corinthians most valued.
9: We know in part, we prophesy in part: the two gifts the Corinthians prized most — knowledge and prophecy — are both partial (ek merous). The honest acknowledgment of epistemological limitation counters the Corinthian gnōsis-boast of superior knowledge.
10: When the perfect (to teleion) comes: to teleion (the complete, the mature, the fully realized) has been interpreted as the completion of the NT canon (cessationist reading), the Second Coming (eschatological reading), or mature corporate spiritual life (developmental reading). The contrast with ek merous (partial) favors an eschatological reading: the fullness of the age to come replaces the partial gifts of the present age.
11: When I was a child: the autobiographical illustration from personal developmental history was a common rhetorical device. The analogy maps onto the ages: childhood corresponds to the present age with its partial gifts; adulthood to the eschatological fullness. Paul uses the first person to avoid condescension.
12: Seeing through a mirror dimly (di esoptrou en ainigmati): esoptron (mirror) in the ancient world was polished bronze or silver, not glass — it produced a real but imperfect and indirect image. Ainigma (riddle, enigma) was used for indirect or obscure speech as opposed to direct speech (prosōpon pros prosōpon, face to face — as Moses spoke with God in Numbers 12:8). The contrast is between indirect, mediated knowledge and direct, unmediated encounter.
13: Faith, hope, and love — the greatest is love: the triad of pistis, elpis, and agapē was already established in early Christian catechesis (1 Thessalonians 1:3; 1 Corinthians 5:8; Galatians 5:5-6; Romans 5:1-5). The listing of love as the greatest is not because faith and hope are lesser virtues, but because love alone persists into the eschatological age — faith will be sight, hope will be fulfillment, but love (as the nature of God himself) continues forever.
